topic: Advanced catalogue strategy — what separates good from great
hook: Month 1 you learned what sync is.
cta:
No brief gets fulfilled without these:
→ WAV masters at 24-bit / 48kHz minimum
→ ISRC registered
→ Stems labelled by instrument group (not by track number)
→ BPM and key confirmed
→ Mood and instrumentation tags completed

slides.headline: Month 1 you learned what sync is.

slides.body:
Month 3 question: what separates a catalogue that gets briefs from one that doesn't? ↓ Width means: you have a lot of stuff.
Supervisors can't use "a lot of stuff."

— — — — — — MOODBOARD YOUR CATALOGUE Great catalogue managers don't think in track titles.
They think in placement types. — — — VERSIONING IS A COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE

DEPTH OVER WIDTH Every track should have a primary and secondary placement type.
Supervisors search this way. Speak their language. One track. Multiple submissions.

A supervisor doesn't need 400 tracks.
They need 40 tracks that are deeply documented, consistently available, instantly deliverable. — — — THE TECHNICAL FLOOR

Depth means:
→ Every track has a complete metadata record
→ Every track has stems prepared and labelled
→ Every co-writer credit is documented and signed
→ Rights

slides.headline: Open briefs. Every Tuesday.
slides.body: Composers: apply once, get matched for life. Supervisors: shortlist in 48h.
